cases.title: Jessica Fuller v. Hyde School
cases.incident_description: Plaintiff's counsel used generative AI (Claude or ChatGPT) to draft a response to a motion to dismiss. The AI generated fictitious case citations and inaccurate descriptions of legal authorities, which were submitted to the court without independent verification.
cases.system_architecture: The AI system (Claude or ChatGPT) was developed by a model provider and used by the plaintiff's counsel (deployer/human operator) as a drafting and research aid.
cases.primary_source_citation: Fuller v. Hyde Sch., No. 2:25-cv-00354-SDN (D. Me. May 5, 2026)
cases.quote_supporting_attribution: She relied on generative AI to produce case citations and descriptions but did not confirm that those authorities existed or accurately stated the law before filing.
cases.notes: Court explicitly sanctioned the attorney for failing to verify AI-generated citations. Opposing counsel argument: The AI provider should bear some fault for designing a system that confidently hallucinates legal citations without adequate warnings.
cases.causal_rule_applied: C1a: Hallucination originates in the model; minor human contribution for non-validation

cases.title: Smith v. Verra Mobility Corp.
cases.incident_description: An attorney representing the School Board of Polk County used ChatGPT to research and draft a motion to dismiss. The AI generated fabricated caselaw and misrepresentations of existing cases, which the attorney filed without independent verification.
cases.system_architecture: OpenAI built ChatGPT, which was deployed by an individual attorney (Jeffrey Sullivan) for legal research and drafting.
cases.primary_source_citation: Smith v. Verra Mobility Corp., No. 8:25-cv-2300-KKM-NHA, 2026 WL 1197241 (M.D. Fla. May 1, 2026)
cases.quote_supporting_attribution: By negligently failing to verify multiple authorities cited in his motion to dismiss, Sullivan violated his duty to ensure that all legal contentions in his motion were supported by existing law.
cases.notes: Court explicitly sanctioned the attorney for failing to verify AI output; opposing counsel might argue the AI provider should share fault for generating convincing fake cases, but Rule 11 places the duty entirely on the signing attorney.
cases.causal_rule_applied: C1a: Hallucination originates in the model; minor human contribution for non-validation

cases.title: Sai Malena Jimenez-Fogarty v. Thomas Fogarty et al.
cases.incident_description: Attorney Tricia S. Lindsay filed two memoranda of law in opposition to motions to dismiss that contained numerous fabricated case citations. The citations were generated by an AI program, and the attorney failed to verify their existence or validity before submitting them to the court.
cases.system_architecture: The AI system was used by the attorney for legal research and drafting. The specific AI system is not definitively identified, though the attorney hinted at using Lexis Nexis's AI-driven features.
cases.primary_source_citation: 24 Civ. 8705 (JLR) (GWG)
cases.quote_supporting_attribution: We thus find that Lindsay used an AI program that generated the fabricated citations in the memoranda of law and that Lindsay failed to check that these citations were genuine.
cases.notes: Sanctioned attorney for failing to verify AI-generated citations; opposing counsel might argue the AI provider bears some fault for generating realistic but fake citations without adequate warnings.
cases.causal_rule_applied: C1b: Vertically integrated deployer = causal producer of hallucinating system

Opposing counsel argument: The AI provider should bear some liability because their sales rep allegedly claimed there was 'no way' the program could produce hallucinations.

cases.title: Cartagena v. Dixon
cases.incident_description: Attorney Tyrone Blackburn submitted a brief in support of a motion to dismiss that contained pervasive citations to non-existent authority and misrepresentations of the law. Blackburn claimed the miscited cases resulted from using the AI legal research platform Protégé in LexisNexis, but LexisNexis refuted that he had a subscription to its AI tools.
cases.system_architecture: The attorney claimed to have used LexisNexis's Protégé AI tool for legal research, but the court found he did not actually have access to it.
cases.primary_source_citation: Cartagena v. Dixon, No. 1:25-cv-03552 (JLR) (S.D.N.Y. Mar. 10, 2026)
cases.quote_supporting_attribution: Blackburn does not dispute this and instead states that the miscited cases resulted from using the AI legal research platform Protégé in LexisNexis. Dkt. 58. LexisNexis, however, provided a letter to the Court refuting that Blackburn had a subscription to its AI tools, “Lexis+ AI” or “Protege.” Dkt. 96-1. Thus, the Court is not only concerned with Blackburn’s submission of hallucinated cases and misstatements of the law in his brief, but also with his misrepresentations to the Court regarding the source of his research.
cases.notes: Court admonished attorney for submitting hallucinated cases and misrepresenting his use of LexisNexis AI tools. Opposing counsel argument: The AI provider should be liable for the hallucinations. However, the attorney did not even have access to the tool, so the fault is entirely his.

cases.system_architecture: OpenAI developed ChatGPT. Attorney James Martin Paul and his paralegal used it as a drafting tool for legal filings.
cases.primary_source_citation: Byoplanet International, LLC v. Johansson, No. 0:25-cv-60630 (S.D. Fla. July 15, 2025)
cases.quote_supporting_attribution: "While the use of AI by itself is not inherently suspect, wholesale reliance on AI without further inquiry or diligence by a lawyer is conduct which a court should deter, as lawyers must always conduct a reasonable inquiry."
cases.notes: Sanctions order explicitly holds the attorney responsible for failing to verify AI-generated citations; opposing counsel might argue the AI provider shares fault for generating fake cases, but the court strictly held the attorney liable.
cases.causal_rule_applied: C1a: Hallucination originates in the model; minor human contribution for non-validation

cases.title: Gurpreet Kaur v. Captain Joel Desso
cases.incident_description: An attorney used the generative AI tool Claude Sonnet 4 to draft a supplemental brief in a habeas corpus petition. The AI generated fabricated quotations from real Supreme Court cases, which the attorney submitted to the court without verifying their accuracy.
cases.system_architecture: Anthropic built the Claude Sonnet 4 model. The attorney deployed it as a drafting assistant for legal submissions.

telemetry.deployer_org: Law Office of Dennis F. Desmarais

cases.primary_source_citation: Kaur v. Desso, No. 9:25-cv-726 (AMN) (N.D.N.Y. July 9, 2025)
cases.quote_supporting_attribution: Mr. Desmarais admits that he did not seek to confirm the existence or validity of the legal quotations in his supplemental brief.
cases.notes: Sanctions imposed entirely on the attorney for failing to verify AI-generated citations, establishing human operator as 100% liable. Opposing counsel could argue the AI provider bears some fault for generating realistic but fake quotes, but Rule 11 places the duty strictly on the signing attorney.
cases.causal_rule_applied: C1a: Hallucination originates in the model; minor human contribution for non-validation

cases.title: Northbound Processing v. South African Diamond Regulator

cases.title: D'Angelo v. Vaught
cases.incident_description: An attorney used an AI tool called Archie (developed by Smokeball) to edit and expand his legal pleadings. The AI tool hallucinated a non-existent case citation (Hauter v. DeWitt) and inserted it into two separate court filings.
cases.system_architecture: Smokeball developed the AI tool Archie, which was deployed by the attorney's law firm (SK Walker Law, LLC) to review, edit, and draft legal pleadings.
cases.primary_source_citation: D'Angelo v. Vaught, No. 21-CH-29 (Ill. Cir. Ct. Williamson Cnty. Apr. 2, 2025)
cases.quote_supporting_attribution: Here, there is no doubt that attorney Walker on two occasions cited to the Court a non-existent case. He either (1) failed to proof-read two documents he states he drafted; (2) failed to note that Archie added in a citation he did not intend; (3) failed to understand how Archie works; or (4) intentionally cited to and quoted a non-existent case. Whether he did so intentionally or by negligence is of little moment to the Court. What is significant is that he filed two pleadings which, considering the facts most favorable to him, he failed to proofread.
cases.notes: Verified via court order Apr 2 2025; explicit sanction against attorney for failing to proofread AI-generated content supports human_operator-primary allocation. Opposing counsel could argue the AI provider should share fault for altering citations without clear warning, but the court explicitly held the attorney accountable for the final work product.
cases.causal_rule_applied: C1b: Vertically integrated deployer = causal producer of hallucinating system

cases.title: Louis v. SafeRent Solutions, LLC
cases.incident_description: SafeRent Solutions' tenant-screening algorithm, the 'SafeRent Score', relied heavily on credit history and other non-tenancy debts while failing to account for the financial benefits of housing vouchers. This resulted in disproportionately lower scores and subsequent housing denials for Black, Hispanic, and low-income voucher-holding applicants. Plaintiffs alleged this practice violated the Fair Housing Act and Massachusetts antidiscrimination laws.
cases.system_architecture: SafeRent Solutions designed and sold the 'SafeRent Score' algorithm. Housing providers, such as Metropolitan Management Group, deployed the service to screen rental applicants and relied on its accept/decline recommendations to make leasing decisions.

telemetry.deployment_environment: Tenant screening for rental housing applications

Compl. at ¶ 43].
cases.notes: Verified via D. Mass. 2023 order denying motion to dismiss and 2024 settlement; AI provider held primarily responsible as it effectively controlled the housing decision. telemetry.deployer_org: Wisconsin Department of Corrections / Wisconsin Circuit Court

cases.primary_source_citation: State v. Loomis, 881 N.W.2d 749 (Wis. 2016)
cases.quote_supporting_attribution: Ultimately, we conclude that if used properly as set forth herein, a circuit court's consideration of a COMPAS risk assessment at sentencing does not violate a defendant's right to due process and that the circuit court did not erroneously exercise its discretion here.
cases.notes: The Wisconsin Supreme Court upheld the use of COMPAS at sentencing, placing the responsibility on the deployer (the court) to use it properly with awareness of its limitations; opposing counsel might argue that the proprietary nature of the tool makes the AI provider partially liable for any due process violations.
cases.causal_rule_applied: C8: Government agency as deployer is the proximate causal actor; vendor model contributes

cases.title: Garante per la protezione dei dati personali v. Replika (Luka Inc.)
cases.incident_description: The Italian Data Protection Authority (Garante) issued an urgent order against Luka Inc., the developer of the AI chatbot Replika, for unlawfully processing the personal data of Italian users, particularly minors. The chatbot lacked age verification mechanisms and provided inappropriate responses to children, violating the GDPR.
cases.system_architecture: Luka Inc. developed and deployed the Replika chatbot, an AI-powered virtual companion based on a large language model. The system interacted directly with users via text and voice interfaces.
cases.primary_source_citation: Provvedimento del 2 febbraio 2023 [9852214]
cases.quote_supporting_attribution: ai sensi dell’art. 58, par. 2, lett. f), del Regolamento dispone, in via d’urgenza, nei confronti di Luka Inc, società statunitense sviluppatrice e gestrice di Replika, in qualità di titolare del trattamento dei dati personali effettuato attraverso tale applicazione, la misura della limitazione provvisoria, del trattamento dei dati personali degli utenti stabiliti nel territorio italiano
cases.notes: Verified via Italian Garante urgent order Feb 2 2023; explicit finding that Luka Inc. as data controller violated GDPR by processing minor data without consent or age verification. Opposing counsel argument: The users (or their parents) are at fault for lying about their age or failing to supervise, but the Garante explicitly rejected this by requiring the deployer to implement age verification.
cases.causal_rule_applied: C5: Privacy violations originate in deployer's data-handling policy

cases.title: Garante v. OpenAI (provisional limitation order)
cases.incident_description: The Italian Data Protection Authority (Garante) issued an emergency order temporarily banning OpenAI's ChatGPT in Italy. The Garante found that OpenAI unlawfully collected personal data to train its algorithms without a valid legal basis and failed to implement age verification to protect minors.
cases.system_architecture: OpenAI developed and deployed ChatGPT, a large language model, directly to users.
cases.primary_source_citation: Provvedimento del 30 marzo 2023 [9870832]
cases.quote_supporting_attribution: RILEVATA l’assenza di idonea base giuridica in relazione alla raccolta dei dati personali e al loro trattamento per scopo di addestramento degli algoritmi sottesi al funzionamento di ChatGPT
cases.notes: Verified via Italian Garante order Mar 30 2023; explicit finding of unlawful data collection for training supports ai_provider-primary allocation. Opposing counsel argument: The order is provisional and OpenAI later addressed the concerns, meaning liability wasn't permanently established.
cases.causal_rule_applied: C5: Privacy violations originate in deployer's data-handling policy

cases.title: ICO v Clearview AI Inc
cases.incident_description: Clearview AI scraped billions of facial images from the public internet to create a searchable database, which it provided to foreign law enforcement and national security clients. The UK Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) issued enforcement and penalty notices against Clearview for unlawfully processing the biometric data of UK residents without their knowledge or consent.
cases.system_architecture: Clearview AI developed and maintained the facial recognition database by scraping images and generating facial vectors. Its clients (law enforcement and national security agencies) deployed the system by uploading probe images to search for matches.

telemetry.deployer_org: Foreign law enforcement and national security agencies

cases.quote_supporting_attribution: "The ICO decided that Clearview had infringed Articles 5, 6, 9, 14, 15 to 17, 21 and 22 of the two GDPRs, and had failed to carry out a Data Protection Impact Assessment under Article 35. The ICO imposed a monetary penalty administrative fine on Clearview of £7,552,800" (Page 6, para 8)
cases.notes: The Upper Tribunal confirmed the ICO's jurisdiction to fine Clearview AI for GDPR violations related to scraping UK residents' images. Opposing counsel could argue that Clearview's clients (foreign states) were the actual deployers conducting the behavioural monitoring, and thus Clearview should not bear primary liability under the GDPR's territorial scope.
cases.causal_rule_applied: C5: Privacy violations originate in deployer's data-handling policy

cases.title: CNIL v. Clearview AI (France)
cases.incident_description: Clearview AI scraped billions of images from the internet without consent to build a facial recognition database. The French data protection authority (CNIL) found this violated the GDPR, as the company had no legal basis for processing the biometric data of individuals in France.
cases.system_architecture: Clearview AI developed and deployed a facial recognition software that scraped publicly available images from the internet to create biometric templates. The system was marketed to law enforcement agencies as a search tool.
cases.primary_source_citation: Délibération SAN-2022-019 du 17 octobre 2022
cases.quote_supporting_attribution: La formation restreinte de la CNIL, après en avoir délibéré, décide de : - prononcer à l’encontre de la société […] une amende administrative d’un montant de 20 000 000 (vingt millions) euros
cases.notes: Verified via CNIL deliberation SAN-2022-019; Clearview AI fined €20M for GDPR violations regarding facial recognition data scraping. Opposing counsel argument: Clearview could argue the data was publicly available and thus not subject to the same privacy expectations.
cases.causal_rule_applied: C5: Privacy violations originate in deployer's data-handling policy

cases.title: ICO v. Serco Leisure
cases.incident_description: Serco Leisure used facial recognition and fingerprint scanning technology to monitor employee attendance across 38 leisure facilities. The Information Commissioner's Office found that Serco failed to establish a lawful basis for processing this biometric data, as less intrusive means were available and employees were not given clear options to object.
cases.system_architecture: The facial recognition and fingerprint scanning systems were provided by SWT Software Limited (trading as ShopWorks), acting as a processor, and deployed by Serco Leisure to monitor employee attendance.
cases.primary_source_citation: ICO Enforcement Notice to Serco Leisure Operating Limited (19 Feb 2024)
cases.quote_supporting_attribution: Serco cannot rely on Article 6(1)(f) as it has failed to demonstrate the necessity of the processing or give appropriate weight to the interests of data subjects when conducting the balancing test. Less intrusive means of achieving the results are available.
cases.notes: Verified via ICO enforcement notice Feb 19 2024; explicit finding that Serco failed to establish a lawful basis for processing biometric data supports deployer-primary allocation. Opposing counsel argument: The AI provider (ShopWorks) should bear some liability for providing a system that inherently processes biometric data without built-in consent mechanisms.
cases.causal_rule_applied: C5: Privacy violations originate in deployer's data-handling policy

cases.title: NTSB HWY18FH011 — Walter Huang / Tesla Model X
cases.incident_description: A Tesla Model X operating on Autopilot steered into a highway gore area and impacted a crash attenuator. The driver, who was likely distracted by a cell phone game, did not respond to the system's limitations and died from his injuries.
cases.system_architecture: Tesla developed and deployed the Autopilot system in the Model X. The system provided partial driving automation (Level 2) requiring active driver supervision.
cases.primary_source_citation: NTSB/HAR-20/01
cases.quote_supporting_attribution: The National Transportation Safety Board determines that the probable cause of the Mountain View, California, crash was the Tesla Autopilot system steering the sport utility vehicle into a highway gore area due to system limitations, and the driver’s lack of response due to distraction likely from a cell phone game application and overreliance on the Autopilot partial driving automation system.
cases.notes: NTSB does not assign legal liability, but attributes probable cause to both the Tesla Autopilot system limitations and the driver's distraction/overreliance. Opposing counsel argument: The driver was explicitly warned to maintain attention and keep hands on the wheel, making the human operator 100% liable.
cases.causal_rule_applied: C4a: Integrated system; deployer designed handover, human failed to react

cases.title: NTSB HWY18MH010 — Elaine Herzberg / Uber ATG
cases.incident_description: An automated test vehicle operated by Uber Advanced Technologies Group struck and fatally injured a pedestrian crossing the road midblock at night. The automated driving system failed to correctly classify and predict the path of the pedestrian, and the human operator was visually distracted by her personal cell phone.
cases.system_architecture: Uber Advanced Technologies Group developed and deployed the automated driving system on a modified Volvo XC90 test vehicle.
cases.quote_supporting_attribution: The National Transportation Safety Board determines that the probable cause of the crash in Tempe, Arizona, was the failure of the vehicle operator to monitor the driving environment and the operation of the automated driving system because she was visually distracted throughout the trip by her personal cell phone. Contributing to the crash were the Uber Advanced Technologies Group’s (1) inadequate safety risk assessment procedures, (2) ineffective oversight of vehicle operators, and (3) lack of adequate mechanisms for addressing operators’ automation complacency—all a consequence of its inadequate safety culture.
cases.notes: NTSB report explicitly blames the human operator as the probable cause and Uber ATG as contributing; opposing counsel could argue the pedestrian's impairment and illegal crossing should reduce liability.
cases.causal_rule_applied: C3a: Vertically integrated perception system; deployer is producer

cases.title: DOJ / NHTSA v. Cruise LLC
cases.incident_description: A pedestrian was struck by a human-driven vehicle and propelled into the path of a Cruise autonomous vehicle. The Cruise vehicle braked but collided with the pedestrian, who fell underneath it. The vehicle then resumed movement, dragging the pedestrian for approximately 20 feet before coming to a final stop.
cases.system_architecture: Cruise LLC, a subsidiary of General Motors, developed and deployed the autonomous driving system in its robotaxi fleet.
cases.primary_source_citation: In re: Cruise, LLC Standing General Order 2021-01 Reporting, Consent Order (NHTSA Sept. 26, 2024)
cases.quote_supporting_attribution: After initially coming to a stop after this collision, the Cruise vehicle resumed movement, dragging the pedestrian for approximately 20 feet before coming to a final stop.
cases.notes: Verified via NHTSA consent order Sept 26 2024 and DOJ DPA Nov 14 2024; Cruise penalized for failing to report the dragging incident. Opposing counsel argument: The initial impact was caused by a human driver, so the human driver should bear primary liability for the incident.
cases.causal_rule_applied: C3a: Vertically integrated perception system; deployer is producer

cases.title: NHTSA EA22-002 Tesla Autopilot recall
cases.incident_description: NHTSA investigated incidents involving Tesla vehicles operating with Autosteer engaged. Tesla voluntarily recalled over 2 million vehicles because the prominence and scope of the feature's controls may not be sufficient to prevent driver misuse, leading to an increased risk of collision if the driver fails to maintain continuous responsibility.
cases.system_architecture: Tesla developed and deployed the Autopilot system, including the Autosteer feature, in its vehicles.
cases.primary_source_citation: NHTSA Part 573 Safety Recall Report 23V-838
cases.quote_supporting_attribution: In certain circumstances when Autosteer is engaged, the prominence and scope of the feature’s controls may not be sufficient to prevent driver misuse of the SAE Level 2 advanced driver-assistance feature.
cases.notes: NHTSA recall report states Autosteer controls were insufficient to prevent driver misuse; opposing counsel could argue the driver is ultimately responsible for maintaining control as stated in the manual, but the recall itself places the burden of fixing the system design on Tesla.
cases.causal_rule_applied: C4a: Integrated system; deployer designed handover, human failed to react

cases.title: California PUC suspension of Cruise driverless permits
cases.incident_description: A pedestrian was struck by a human driver and thrown into the path of a Cruise autonomous vehicle. The Cruise AV performed a hard-braking maneuver but ran over the pedestrian, then attempted a pullover maneuver while the pedestrian was still underneath, dragging them for 20 feet at 7 mph.
cases.system_architecture: Cruise LLC built and deployed the autonomous driving stack in its driverless robotaxis operating in San Francisco.

telemetry.deployment_environment: Public roads in San Francisco, California

cases.primary_source_citation: CPUC Decision 24-07-004 (July 25, 2024)
cases.quote_supporting_attribution: The Cruise AV travelled approximately 20 feet and reached a speed of 7 mph before coming to a complete stop. During this pullover, the pedestrian remained lodged under the Cruise AV.
cases.notes: Verified via CPUC Decision 24-07-004 approving settlement for failing to report the full extent of the incident. Opposing counsel argument: The initial collision was caused by a human driver, so the AI system was not the primary cause of the incident.
cases.causal_rule_applied: C1b: Vertically integrated deployer = causal producer of hallucinating system

cases.title: Williams v. City of Detroit
cases.incident_description: Robert Williams was wrongfully arrested by the Detroit Police Department based on a flawed facial recognition match. The system misidentified him from a low-quality surveillance image, and officers relied on this match without adequate independent investigation.
cases.system_architecture: The Detroit Police Department used facial recognition technology provided by DataWorks, which ran algorithms from Rank One Computing or NEC, to generate investigative leads.
cases.primary_source_citation: Williams v. City of Detroit, No. 21-cv-10827 (E.D. Mich. June 28, 2024)
cases.quote_supporting_attribution: "The parties, through their respective counsel, hereby stipulate and agree as follows: 1. Plaintiff and Defendants have reached a negotiated resolution in this matter. To that end, the parties have entered into a Settlement Agreement."
cases.notes: Verified via settlement agreement Jun 28 2024; explicit policy changes and $300,000 payout support deployer-primary allocation. Opposing counsel argument: The AI provider should bear some liability for providing a system with known racial biases.
cases.causal_rule_applied: C3b: Perception defect is in the model; deployer contributes via integration

cases.title: Woodruff v. Oliver
cases.incident_description: Porcha Woodruff, an eight-months pregnant woman, was wrongfully arrested for carjacking and armed robbery based on a flawed facial recognition match by the Detroit Police Department. The facial recognition system matched an eight-year-old mugshot of Woodruff to low-quality surveillance footage of a suspect who was not pregnant.
cases.system_architecture: The Detroit Police Department deployed a facial recognition system to identify suspects from surveillance footage. An investigator ran the search, which returned Woodruff as a lead, and another officer used this lead to conduct a photo lineup and obtain an arrest warrant.
cases.primary_source_citation: Woodruff v. Oliver, No. 23-11886 (E.D. Mich. Aug. 5, 2025)
cases.quote_supporting_attribution: The DPD has since changed its policies to ban the practice of seeking an arrest warrant 'solely on the basis of an investigative lead developed through Facial Recognition technology in combination with a lineup identification' without 'additional independent reliable evidence.'
cases.notes: The court granted summary judgment to the defendant officer on false arrest claims, but the DPD changed its policies regarding FRT use, supporting deployer liability. Opposing counsel could argue the officer's failure to investigate (human operator error) was the primary cause, not the AI deployment itself.
cases.causal_rule_applied: C9: Upstream data-quality breakdown drives the downstream failure

cases.title: FTC v. Rite Aid Corp.
cases.incident_description: Rite Aid deployed facial recognition technology in its retail stores to identify individuals it previously deemed likely to engage in shoplifting. The system generated thousands of false-positive match alerts, disproportionately affecting Black, Asian, Latino, and women consumers, leading employees to wrongly accuse, search, and remove consumers from stores.
cases.system_architecture: Rite Aid obtained facial recognition technology from two third-party vendors and deployed it in hundreds of retail pharmacy locations. Store-level employees operated the technology and received match alerts on mobile devices.
cases.primary_source_citation: Complaint, FTC v. Rite Aid Corp., No. 2:23-cv-5023 (E.D. Pa. Dec. 19, 2023)
cases.quote_supporting_attribution: Rite Aid failed to take reasonable measures to prevent harm to consumers from its use of facial recognition technology. Among other things, Rite Aid failed to consider or address foreseeable harms to consumers flowing from its use of facial recognition technology, failed to test or assess the technology’s accuracy before or after deployment, failed to enforce image quality standards that were necessary for the technology to function accurately, and failed to take reasonable steps to train and oversee the employees charged with operating the technology in Rite Aid stores.
cases.notes: Verified via FTC complaint Dec 19 2023; explicit focus on Rite Aid's failure to test, train, and enforce quality controls supports deployer-primary allocation. Opposing counsel could argue the AI provider bears some fault for supplying a flawed system, but the FTC explicitly targeted Rite Aid's deployment practices.
cases.causal_rule_applied: C2a: Deployer trained or configured the discriminatory system

cases.title: FTC v. DoNotPay, Inc.
cases.incident_description: DoNotPay marketed its service as 'the world's first robot lawyer' and claimed its AI could substitute for human legal expertise, such as drafting demand letters and initiating small claims court cases. The FTC alleged these claims were false and unsubstantiated, as the service was not trained on comprehensive legal data and its outputs were not tested by attorneys.
cases.system_architecture: DoNotPay developed the service using natural language processing, chatbot software, and an API with OpenAI's ChatGPT. The service was deployed directly to consumers via a website and mobile app.
cases.primary_source_citation: In the Matter of DoNotPay, Inc., FTC Docket No. C-4812 (Jan. 14, 2025)
cases.quote_supporting_attribution: Respondent used the emergence of new technology like artificial intelligence (AI) as a marketing tool, positioning the DoNotPay Service as a cutting-edge solution for producing legal documents. Respondent described the Service as 'the world’s first robot lawyer' and an 'AI lawyer' capable of performing legal services
cases.notes: Verified via FTC complaint and decision/order Jan 14 2025; deployer-primary allocation supported by FTC holding DoNotPay solely liable for deceptive marketing of its AI capabilities. Opposing counsel argument: OpenAI should bear some liability for providing the underlying model that enabled the deceptive claims.
cases.causal_rule_applied: C7: Deceptive marketing is a deployer-side communication failure

cases.title: FTC v. Cleo AI
cases.incident_description: Cleo AI operated a personal finance mobile application that promised consumers instant or same-day cash advances of hundreds of dollars. However, Cleo used an artificial intelligence risk classifier scoring system to limit cash advances to below the advertised amounts, and almost no one received the advertised dollar amount.
cases.system_architecture: Cleo AI built and deployed an artificial intelligence risk classifier scoring system within its personal finance mobile application to determine consumer eligibility and cash advance amounts.
cases.primary_source_citation: Federal Trade Commission v. Cleo AI, Inc., Case No. 1:25-cv-02594 (S.D.N.Y. Mar. 27, 2025)
cases.quote_supporting_attribution: During this process Cleo uses an artificial intelligence risk classifier scoring system [redacted] Cleo withholds the determined cash advance amount from consumers, only informing them whether they are “eligible for an advance.”
cases.notes: Verified via FTC complaint Mar 27 2025; Cleo AI used an AI risk classifier to determine cash advance amounts while deceptively advertising higher amounts. Opposing counsel could argue the AI was functioning as designed and the failure was purely in marketing, not an AI failure per se.
cases.causal_rule_applied: C7: Deceptive marketing is a deployer-side communication failure

cases.title: FTC v. Evolv Technologies
cases.incident_description: Evolv Technologies deceptively marketed its AI-powered weapons detection system, Evolv Express, claiming it could detect all weapons and ignore harmless items. In reality, the system failed to detect a knife used in a school stabbing and frequently triggered false alarms on common items like umbrellas and lunchboxes.
cases.system_architecture: Evolv Technologies built and marketed the Evolv Express AI-powered security scanners. The scanners were deployed by various customers, including schools, stadiums, and hospitals, to screen individuals entering their premises.
cases.primary_source_citation: FTC v. Evolv Technologies Holdings, Inc., Case No. 1:24-cv-12940 (D. Mass. Nov. 26, 2024)
cases.quote_supporting_attribution: Defendant has made false or unsupported claims about its security screening system, Evolv Express ("Express"), in use at the entries of thousands of schools and other venues across the country.
cases.notes: Verified via FTC complaint and stipulated order Nov 26 2024; 100% allocation to AI provider for deceptive marketing claims about system capabilities. Opposing counsel argument: Deployers (schools) should bear some fault for failing to independently verify the system's efficacy before relying on it for physical security.
cases.causal_rule_applied: C7: Deceptive marketing is a deployer-side communication failure

cases.title: FTC v. IntelliVision Technologies
cases.incident_description: IntelliVision Technologies made false, misleading, or unsubstantiated claims that its AI-powered facial recognition software was free of gender or racial bias and had one of the highest accuracy rates on the market. The software was integrated into consumer products like home security systems and smart home touch panels.
cases.system_architecture: IntelliVision Technologies developed the facial recognition software. It was deployed by original equipment manufacturers (OEMs) and its former parent corporation Nice North America, LLC, integrating it into consumer products like the 2GIG Edge home security system and Elan Intelligent Touch Panel.

telemetry.deployer_org: Nice North America, LLC and other OEMs
telemetry.deployment_environment: Consumer home security systems and smart home touch panels

cases.primary_source_citation: In the Matter of IntelliVision Technologies Corp., FTC Matter/File Number 232 3023 (2025)
cases.quote_supporting_attribution: Respondent does not possess testing to support its claims that its facial recognition technology has one of the highest accuracy rates on the market, that it can detect faces of all ethnicities without racial bias, or that it performs with zero gender or racial bias.
cases.notes: Verified via FTC complaint and consent order Jan 13 2025; explicit findings of deceptive marketing support ai_provider-primary allocation. Opposing counsel argument: Deployers like Nice North America should share liability for failing to independently verify the accuracy and bias claims before integrating the software into consumer products.
cases.causal_rule_applied: C7: Deceptive marketing is a deployer-side communication failure

cases.title: FTC v. accessiBe
cases.incident_description: accessiBe marketed its AI-powered web accessibility tool, accessWidget, claiming it could make any website fully compliant with the Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG) for people with disabilities. The FTC alleged these claims were false, misleading, or unsubstantiated, as the tool failed to make basic website components compliant.
cases.system_architecture: accessiBe built and deployed accessWidget, an AI-powered software plug-in, which website owners integrated into their sites to purportedly achieve WCAG compliance.
cases.primary_source_citation: In the Matter of accessiBe Inc., FTC Docket No. C-4817 (Apr. 21, 2025)
cases.quote_supporting_attribution: IT IS ORDERED that Respondents... must not make any representation, expressly or by implication: A. That such product or service, including accessWidget's artificial intelligence and other automated technology, can make any website compliant with WCAG
cases.notes: Verified via FTC Decision and Order Apr 21 2025; 100% liability to AI provider for deceptive marketing of its AI accessibility tool. Opposing counsel argument: The deployers (website owners) should bear some liability for failing to independently verify their websites' WCAG compliance.
cases.causal_rule_applied: C7: Deceptive marketing is a deployer-side communication failure

cases.title: Walters v. OpenAI, L.L.C.
cases.incident_description: A journalist used ChatGPT to summarize a federal lawsuit. ChatGPT falsely stated that the lawsuit accused Mark Walters, a radio host, of embezzling funds from the Second Amendment Foundation.
cases.system_architecture: OpenAI developed and deployed the ChatGPT large language model, which was accessed directly by the user via the internet.
cases.primary_source_citation: Walters v. OpenAI, L.L.C., No. 23-A-04860-2 (Ga. Super. Ct. Gwinnett Cnty. May 19, 2025)
cases.quote_supporting_attribution: The Court finds that the challenged output does not communicate defamatory meaning as a matter of law. This alone is sufficient to require summary judgment in favor of OpenAI.
cases.notes: Verified via Georgia Superior Court summary judgment May 19 2025; court found no defamatory meaning as a matter of law because a reasonable reader would not believe the output stated actual facts. Opposing counsel argument: The output was literally false and accused plaintiff of a crime, which is defamation per se, and the disclaimers were insufficient to overcome the plain meaning of the text.
cases.causal_rule_applied: C1b: Vertically integrated deployer = causal producer of hallucinating system

cases.title: Moffatt v. Air Canada
cases.incident_description: A customer used an airline's website chatbot to inquire about bereavement fares. The chatbot incorrectly stated that the customer could apply for a retroactive refund within 90 days, contradicting the airline's actual policy which required advance approval.
cases.system_architecture: Air Canada deployed a customer service chatbot on its website to answer user queries. The chatbot provided automated responses to customer inquiries.
cases.primary_source_citation: Moffatt v. Air Canada, 2024 BCCRT 149
cases.quote_supporting_attribution: I find Air Canada did not take reasonable care to ensure its chatbot was accurate. While Air Canada argues Mr. Moffatt could find the correct information on another part of its website, it does not explain why the webpage titled “Bereavement travel” was inherently more trustworthy than its chatbot.
cases.notes: Verified via BCCRT decision Feb 14 2024; explicit finding of negligent misrepresentation supports deployer-primary allocation. Opposing counsel might argue the user was negligent for not clicking the provided hyperlink to the actual policy, but the tribunal explicitly rejected this.
cases.causal_rule_applied: C1b: Vertically integrated deployer = causal producer of hallucinating system

cases.title: Thomson Reuters Enterprise Centre GmbH v. ROSS Intelligence Inc.
cases.incident_description: ROSS Intelligence trained its AI legal research tool using 'Bulk Memos' created by LegalEase, which were in turn built from Westlaw headnotes. Thomson Reuters sued ROSS for copyright infringement.
cases.system_architecture: ROSS Intelligence built and deployed an AI search tool trained on data provided by LegalEase.
cases.primary_source_citation: Thomson Reuters Enter. Ctr. GmbH v. Ross Intel. Inc., No. 1:20-cv-613-SB (D. Del. Feb. 11, 2025)
cases.quote_supporting_attribution: Ross built its competing product using Bulk Memos, which in turn were built from Westlaw headnotes.
cases.notes: Court granted partial summary judgment to Thomson Reuters for direct copyright infringement. Opposing counsel could argue that LegalEase was the actual copier and ROSS was just the deployer.
cases.causal_rule_applied: C6: IP infringement originates in model training data and outputs

cases.title: R (Bridges) v. Chief Constable of South Wales Police
cases.incident_description: The South Wales Police deployed automated facial recognition (AFR) technology called AFR Locate in public spaces. The claimant challenged the deployment, arguing that the police failed to adequately assess whether the software had a racial or gender bias, thereby breaching the Public Sector Equality Duty.
cases.system_architecture: The South Wales Police licensed proprietary AFR software (NeoFace Watch) from NEC. The police deployed it using CCTV cameras and compared extracted facial biometrics against watchlists compiled by the police.
cases.primary_source_citation: [2020] EWCA Civ 1058
cases.quote_supporting_attribution: The fact remains, however, that SWP have never sought to satisfy themselves, either directly or by way of independent verification, that the software program in this case does not have an unacceptable bias on grounds of race or sex. ... it does not enable a public authority to discharge its own, non-delegable, duty under section 149.
cases.notes: Court held deployer (SWP) 100% liable for failing its non-delegable Public Sector Equality Duty to verify the AI lacked racial/gender bias. Opposing counsel argument: The AI provider (NEC) refused to share training data due to commercial confidentiality, making it impossible for the deployer to verify bias, so the provider should bear some fault.
cases.causal_rule_applied: C2b: Bias originates in training; deployer shares causal role for failing to validate fairness

cases.title: NJCM v. State of the Netherlands (SyRI judgment)
cases.incident_description: The Dutch government used a system called SyRI to link and analyze data from various government bodies to predict the risk of social security and tax fraud. Civil society groups sued, arguing the system violated the right to privacy under Article 8 of the European Convention on Human Rights.
cases.system_architecture: The system was developed and deployed by the Dutch government (Ministry of Social Affairs and Employment) in collaboration with other government bodies.
cases.primary_source_citation: ECLI:NL:RBDHA:2020:1878
cases.quote_supporting_attribution: The court has decided that this legislation does not comply with Article 8 of the European Convention on Human Rights (ECHR), which protects the right to respect for private and family life, home and correspondence.
cases.notes: Verified via Hague District Court judgment Feb 5 2020; explicit ruling that SyRI legislation violates ECHR Article 8 supports deployer-primary allocation. Opposing counsel argument: The system was not an AI system but a simple decision tree, so AI liability frameworks should not apply.
cases.causal_rule_applied: C5: Privacy violations originate in deployer's data-handling policy

cases.title: Robodebt — Federal Court class-action
cases.incident_description: The Commonwealth of Australia used an automated debt-collection system, colloquially known as the 'Robodebt system', to recover social security payments it alleged had been overpaid. The system attempted to identify overpayments by automatically matching PAYG income information from the Australian Taxation Office and evenly apportioning that income over fortnightly increments, comparing it with actual fortnightly income information provided by the person to Centrelink.
cases.system_architecture: The Commonwealth designed and deployed the automated Robodebt system to identify overpayments of social security benefits through data matching and income averaging.
cases.primary_source_citation: Prygodicz v Commonwealth of Australia (No 2) [2021] FCA 634
cases.quote_supporting_attribution: In the course of the proceeding the Commonwealth admitted that it did not have a proper legal basis to raise, demand or recover asserted debts which were based on income averaging from ATO data.
cases.notes: Verified via Federal Court of Australia consent orders Jun 11 2021; Commonwealth admitted lack of legal basis for income averaging. Opposing counsel argument: The system was merely a data-matching tool, not an AI system, and the fault lies with the policy design rather than the technology itself.
cases.causal_rule_applied: C8: Government agency as deployer is the proximate causal actor; vendor model contributes

schema_changes_v0_3.purpose: Separate causal ground-truth axis distinct from legal liability_allocation
